text: The surgeon achieves pneumoperitoneum using the Hassan technique, which involves inserting a trocar directly into the abdominal cavity to inflate it with gas for better visualization.
The right side of the abdomen shows a grossly dilated colon, with minimal space visible between the colon and the abdominal wall.
The surgeon releases the attempted sealing of ischemic patches by the abdominal wall using blunt dissection, revealing the underlying tissue.
The surgeon dissects adhesions from a previous midline laparotomy, carefully separating the fibrous tissue bands that are visible in the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon visually identifies the stricturing cancer within the colon, highlighting the affected area with a visible marker.
The surgeon fashions the future ileostomy site to create more space in the abdominal cavity, providing additional room for surgical manipulation.
The surgeon uses an Alexis bag to deliver the anterior cecal wall, revealing the structure within the surgical field.
A Foley's catheter is inserted to deflate the bowel, removing air and liquid feces from the intestinal lumen.
The surgeon uses a green lid to maintain pneumoperitoneum during the procedure, ensuring the abdominal cavity remains inflated for optimal visibility and access.
Following bowel deflation, a noticeable space is created between the bowel and the abdominal wall, enhancing the surgical field visibility.
The surgeon begins medial dissection after identifying the right ureter, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon identifies the left ureter and divides the inferior mesenteric artery using haemlocks, ensuring clear separation of the artery from surrounding tissues.
After lateral mobilization, the surgeon divides the mesorectum, revealing the posterior wall of the upper rectum.
The surgeon divides the upper rectum using an endostapler, completing the transection of this section of the bowel.
The surgical team performs a table lavage, flushing the surgical area with ten liters of warm normal saline.
The inflow of fluid is directed through a Foley catheter, which is visibly positioned within the surgical field.
The outflow of fluid is visibly exiting through the elephant tube connected to the proximal bowel.
The surgeon demonstrates the midline scar on the patient's abdomen, which is visible as a linear mark along the midline of the surgical field.
The video shows an inside view of the outflow limb, likely connected to the elephant tube as previously described.
The surgeon observes the bowel, noting that it is not under tension, indicating a relaxed state of the tissue.
A large, thirty-three circular stapler is shown unable to reach the proximal rectum during the procedure.
The surgeon performs an end-to-side anastomosis to the mid rectum, connecting the bowel segments with visible precision.
The surgeon performs an air insufflation test on the anastomosis, confirming there is no visible leak.
The surgeon clips the appendices epiploica to the fallopian tube to reduce tension on the anastomosis, visually demonstrating the connection between these structures.
The surgeon performs a covering loop ileostomy, creating a temporary stoma by bringing a loop of the ileum through the abdominal wall.
